text.title: Go Error Handling Rules
text.summary: Wrapping rule, sentinel definition, errors.Is/As usage, log-once boundary rule, and error taxonomy.

rule.statement: Wrap every error with the operation name using fmt.Errorf("operation_name: %w", err). Use %w not %v — %v formats the message but does not wrap, making errors.Is/As traversal impossible.
rule.rationale: Without wrapping, a bare "sql: no rows" error from a repository leaks implementation details to callers and provides no call-stack context in logs. The operation name ("user_create: %w") tells you where the failure occurred without a stack trace.

example.code: // %v — formats but does not wrap, errors.Is cannot traverse this return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed: %v", err)

rule.statement: Define all sentinel errors in one apperror package per service. Repository layer wraps low-level errors (sql.ErrNoRows) into domain sentinels (ErrNotFound). Business logic and handlers check domain sentinels only — never raw sql or network error types.
rule.rationale: Letting sql.ErrNoRows leak into business logic couples the domain to the persistence layer. Swapping the DB driver or adding a cache layer then breaks every sentinel check throughout the codebase.

rule.statement: Always use errors.Is to compare sentinel errors and errors.As to extract typed errors. Never use == to compare errors — it fails if any intermediate wrapper does not implement Is().
rule.rationale: Comparing wrapped errors with == is a silent bug: err == apperror.ErrNotFound returns false if the error was wrapped with fmt.Errorf. errors.Is traverses the entire chain.

rule.statement: Log errors exactly once, at the outermost boundary (HTTP handler, gRPC handler, CLI entry point). Intermediate layers wrap and return. Never log-and-return — it produces duplicate Sentry/log events for the same error.
rule.rationale: Each log line creates a Sentry event. Log-and-return in a 3-layer call chain creates 3 events for one failure, making alert counts unreliable and investigation noisy.

rule.statement: For validation or batch operations where multiple errors must be collected, use errors.Join (Go 1.20+) to aggregate them. Do not concatenate error strings manually — the result is not unwrappable.
rule.rationale: String concatenation produces a new error that errors.Is cannot inspect. errors.Join preserves individual errors so callers can check for specific members.

section.title: Transport Mapping Rule

rule.statement: Map domain errors to HTTP status codes or gRPC codes only at the transport edge (handler layer). Business logic must not import net/http or google.golang.org/grpc/codes.
rule.rationale: Transport-specific codes in business logic couple domain to delivery mechanism, making services untestable without an HTTP server and impossible to reuse across protocols.

section.reference: Sentinel errors, AppError type, HTTP mapping, constructor functions
golangci-lint config with errcheck, errorlint, wrapcheck, nilerr
